title: Optimization
overview: Optimization means finding the best solution among many possible options.

blocks.title: What is Optimization & Hill Climbing
blocks.body: Optimization means finding the best solution among many possible options. Hill Climbing is the simplest approach — always move toward a better nearby option.
bullets: Optimization = finding the best solution (cheapest, fastest, most accurate)
Real example: finding the cheapest flight with fewest stops
Hill Climbing: start somewhere, always step toward a better neighbor
Problem: Hill Climbing can get stuck at a local best that is not the global best

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Climbing a hill in thick fog
leftPanel.description: you can only see one step ahead, always step upward

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent: Current state → Check neighbors → Move to best neighbor → Repeat

blocks.question: Can Hill Climbing always find the absolute best solution?
blocks.answer: No, it can get stuck at a local best.

blocks.title: Simulated Annealing & Constraint Satisfaction
blocks.body: Simulated Annealing fixes Hill Climbing by occasionally accepting a worse option to escape being stuck. Constraint Satisfaction Problems have rules the solution must follow.
bullets: Simulated Annealing: sometimes accept a worse option — like shaking a puzzle to unstick pieces
As time goes on, accept worse options less often (temperature cools down)
CSP: a problem where the solution must satisfy a set of constraints (rules)
CSP example: exam scheduling — no student should have two exams at the same time
Sudoku is a classic CSP — every row, column, box must have digits 1-9 with no repeats

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Exam timetable
leftPanel.description: must schedule all exams so no student has a clash

rightPanel.label: Code Example
rightPanel.code: # Sudoku: each row must contain 1-9 with no repeats

blocks.question: Is Sudoku an example of a Constraint Satisfaction Problem?
blocks.answer: Yes, the digits must satisfy strict row, column, and box constraints.

blocks.title: Optimization Steps
bullets: Step 1: Define your problem — what are you trying to optimize?
Step 3: Check neighboring solutions — is any better?
Step 4: Move to the better solution (Hill Climbing) or sometimes move to worse (Simulated Annealing)
Step 5: Repeat until no improvement found

blocks.title: Practice Exercise
blocks.instructions: Solve a simple Sudoku and write which constraint (rule) you used at each step
blocks.hint: Follow row, column, and block constraints.

keyTakeaways.text: Optimization finds the best solution from many possible options
Hill Climbing always moves toward a better nearby option
Hill Climbing can get stuck at a local best — not always the global best
Simulated Annealing escapes by occasionally accepting worse options
CSP problems have constraints (rules) the solution must satisfy
Sudoku, exam scheduling, and delivery routing are all optimization problems

whatYouLearned: How Hill Climbing and Simulated Annealing work
What CSP means and how it applies to Sudoku
How to define states and neighbors
